coredata.dc:title: Validation of specific cation partitioning to molecular surfaces using fluorescent carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Rana, Shameema
Kherb, Jaibir
coredata.dc:description: Specific salt effects are significantly involved in the aqueous colloidal solutions of proteins, nucleic acids, surfactants and nanomaterials. The mechanism of such effects has been systematically investigated here with a simple nanomaterial-based model system. Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots were synthesized for this purpose. This research work highlights the unique advantage offered by fluorescent carbon quantum dots to be utilized as a sensing probe for the detection of selective ion interactions based on their fluorescence turn-on response. Synthesized carbon quantum dots exhibited surface potential dependent strong fluorescence emission. It was further found that the functional group chemistry on the carbon quantum dot surface plays a crucial role in the ion partitioning. A remarkable enhancement of emission intensity observed in the presence of metal cations can be attributed to their relative tendency to form ion-pairs with the nanomaterial surface functionalities. Carbon quantum dots with deprotonated carboxylic groups on their surface showed a stronger affinity for strongly hydrated cations as compared to weakly hydrated cations. Uncharged yet polar nanomaterial surface, however, showed no selectivity towards different ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colloidal particle surface
Fluorescence
Lyotropic effects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from malonic acid
Interaction of cations with carbon quantum dots
Structural and chemical analysis of CQDs
Fluorescence data of CQDs with chloride salts
Fitting of the fluorescence binding data

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) XRD spectra and (d) SEM image of the synthesized CQDs.
(a) XPS wide range spectrum, the high-resolution C1s (b) and O1s (c) spectra of malonic acid CQDs, and (d) zeta potential measurement of CQDs.
(a) UV–Visible absorption spectra (inset: CQDs solution under sunlight and 365 nm UV light illumination) and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs.
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of (a) magnesium chloride and (b) lithium chloride solutions prepared in 10 mM tris buffer.
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of (a) 50 mM concentration of divalent and (b) 1 M of monovalent chloride solutions prepared in 10 mM tris buffer.
Fluorescence intensity response of CQDs with increment in concentration of various divalent and monovalent metal chloride salts.
Plot of Kd versus ΔHhyd (Cation-Acetate) for (a) monovalent and (b) divalent metal chlorides.
Binding parameter values obtained from the fitting of fluorescence data of CQDs in the presence of salts employing Eq. (1).

head.ce:title: Validation of specific cation partitioning to molecular surfaces using fluorescent carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur, Himachal Pradesh 177005, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur, Himachal Pradesh – 177005, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Specific salt effects are significantly involved in the aqueous colloidal solutions of proteins, nucleic acids, surfactants and nanomaterials. The mechanism of such effects has been systematically investigated here with a simple nanomaterial-based model system. Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots were synthesized for this purpose. This research work highlights the unique advantage offered by fluorescent carbon quantum dots to be utilized as a sensing probe for the detection of selective ion interactions based on their fluorescence turn-on response. Synthesized carbon quantum dots exhibited surface potential dependent strong fluorescence emission. It was further found that the functional group chemistry on the carbon quantum dot surface plays a crucial role in the ion partitioning. A remarkable enhancement of emission intensity observed in the presence of metal cations can be attributed to their relative tendency to form ion-pairs with the nanomaterial surface functionalities. Carbon quantum dots with deprotonated carboxylic groups on their surface showed a stronger affinity for strongly hydrated cations as compared to weakly hydrated cations. Uncharged yet polar nanomaterial surface, however, showed no selectivity towards different ions.

ce:section.ce:para:
Specific salt effects on water solubilized molecules play a very vital role in their overall stability, properties and functions. Such interactions are of major importance in numerous scientific fields and processes such as colloidal stability, protein aggregation diseases, cellular ion channels, enzymatic activity and nanomaterial systems [1–4]. Franz Hofmeister and co-workers were among the first researchers to systematically record specific ion effects on biomolecule aggregation. They examined the efficiency of various salts to precipitate out proteins from egg white and blood serum samples [5]. As a result of their pioneering work, for salts having the same anion, the sequencing of the cations in order of their capability to precipitate proteins from aqueous solution was termed as cationic Hofmeister series or cationic lyotropic series:
CH 3
4
N +
>
NH 4
+
>
Cs +
>
K +
>
Na +
>
Li +
>
Ca
2 +
>
Mg
2 +
>
Al
3 +

Similarly, for salts with common cation, the anions follow the below trend:
CO 3
2 –
>
SO 4
2 −
>
H 2
PO 4
−
>
CH 3
COO –
>
F −
>
Cl −
>
Br −
>
NO 3
−
>
I −
>
ClO 4
−
>
SCN −

High charge density anions such as carbonate and sulphate are termed as kosmotropes whereas more hydrophobic anions such as iodide and thiocyanate are classified as chaotropes. This unique terminology was assigned due to their possible impact on the network of water molecules in the aqueous medium. Kosmotropic anions, having a strong affinity for water molecules, are also known as ‘water structure makers’ whereas chaotropic anions are widely accepted as ‘water structure breakers’. According to this ideology, the kosmotropic anions should remove water from the protein's primary structure and hence leading to a stabilizing effect on proteins. On the other hand, chaotropes, being weakly hydrated, should destabilize folded proteins [6]. Hence, the anions before chloride salt-out the proteins while those after chloride generally salt proteins into the solution [7]. Interestingly, this trend gets reversed for the cations. Weakly hydrated chaotropic cations like tetramethylammonium ion present on the left side of the series are found to be most effective at salting-out proteins. On the other hand, the presence of kosmotropic cations in solutions such as Mg2+, leads to enhanced solubilization of proteins [8,9]. Sodium ion presumably has no strong interaction with either solute or solvent and therefore it is present at the center point of this series. Additionally, the specific effects of cations have been found to be much weaker than those of anions which have been ascribed to their lower interaction affinity towards water [9]. These inherently weak yet very important cation specific interactions have been difficult to measure and interpret. Impact of salt addition on bulk water properties such as a change in activity, viscosity and surface tension doesn't completely explain the selectivity observed in these trends. Widely accepted theories regarding electrolytes and their interactions with charged molecular surfaces like Debye-Huckel theory, DLVO theory have also failed to provide clear answers [10]. The experimental observations in most systems get diluted by strong response from the abundant solvent molecules and functional heterogeneity in solubilized macromolecules. Mechanistic information regarding these specific ionic interactions can be fully quantified with the use of techniques that focuses on the molecular surface. Vibrational Sum Frequency Spectroscopy (VSFS) and fluorescence spectroscopy hold great potential in this aspect.

With the advent of high-resolution imaging methods and synthetic protocols, a huge variety of nanomaterials has been discovered over the last three decades. Among these, carbon-based nanomaterials have gained immense trust due to their economical synthesis, biocompatible nature and eco-friendly disposal. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and nanoparticles are the most fascinating member in this class of nanomaterials due to their distinct properties and vast applicability. CQDs belong to zero-dimensional structures that are composed of graphitic nanoparticles having a diameter of 10 nm or less [11,12]. These were first discovered by Xu et al. in 2004 as a side-product during the arc discharge method for the preparation of fluorescent carbon nanotubes [13]. In general, CQDs are described as amorphous or crystalline carbogenic nanomaterials which comprise single or multi-layers of sp2-hybridized hexagonally arranged carbon atoms giving rise to a graphitic core [14,15]. Furthermore, carbon dot's surface contains numerous polar functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl which enrich them with good water dispersibility and also provide an ideal platform for further functionalization with various organic, inorganic, polymeric or biological species [16,17]. High aqueous solubility, strong fluorescence, availability of low cost, simple and green methods of synthesis, the abundance of raw material in nature, robust chemical inertness, high photo-bleaching resistance, non-toxicity are some of the advantages over typical heavy metal/semiconductor-based nanoparticles [18,19]. In addition, CQDs are also relatively easy to functionalize [20] and thus have promising applications in sensing [21,22], bioimaging [23,24], drug delivery [25], catalysis including photocatalysis [26] and electrocatalysis [27], and energy conversion/storage devices [28]. Conventional bottom-up approaches produce CQDs from simple carbon-containing molecules such as citric acid and carbohydrates through microwave synthesis [29], thermal carbonization [30], hydrothermal treatment [31] and plasma treatment [32]. In general, the synthesis of CQDs involves oxidation, so a significant amount of oxygen-containing groups like COOH, CO, and OH are introduced in their molecular structure [33]. The presence of these groups on CQDs surface endows them with unique optical properties, water solubility and ligation capability [34].

In this research work, we have used a fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial system to investigate the detailed mechanism of ionic interactions with macromolecular surfaces. Highly fluorescent carbon dots were firstly synthesized by the thermal carbonization of malonic acid. Synthesized carbon dots were found to have a high degree of acidic functionalities. Striking differences in fluorescence were observed on the incubation of CQDs with different metal chlorides. Moreover, we discuss the specific nature of binding of different cations with the carboxylate moiety present on the nanomaterial surface. The possible role of ion-pairing in inducing partitioning affinity to surfaces is systematically examined here. All in all, we have used a water-soluble, fluorescent carbon nanomaterial to elaborate on the mechanism of various cation's interaction with molecular surfaces. These results will certainly help in the creation of better sensors for ionic pollutants.

Malonic acid, tris buffer and chloride salts of alkali and alkaline earth metal (purity ≥99%) were purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. All procured chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as such. Freshly prepared nanopure water was utilized for preparing all salt and buffer solutions throughout this research study.

UV–Visible absorption spectra were collected on a T80+™ (PG Instruments Ltd) UV/VIS Spectrometer. Fluorescence measurements of carbon nanomaterial solutions were carried out on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC Spectrofluorophotometer. Centrifugation and ultrasonication were carried out using a REMI C-30BL centrifuge (REMI Sales and Engineering Ltd.) and ultrasonic bath (PCI Analytics Pvt. Ltd.) respectively. FTIR vibrational spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer make L1600312 spectrometer in the range 4000–500 cm−1 using transmission mode of measurement. Zeta potential measurements of nanomaterial surface were performed using a Malvern Zeta Sizer Nano-ZS series instrument. Powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD) pattern of dry nanomaterial sample was collected on a Rigaku Miniflex diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) results were acquired using XPS with Auger Electron Spectroscopy (AES) Module (PHI 5000 Versa Probe II, FEI Inc.). Surface morphology and elemental analysis were performed using a JEOL JEM-2100 Plus electron microscope and a JSM-6610 scanning electron microscope equipped with an EDX attachment respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from malonic acid
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized using malonic acid as the carbon precursor. In the synthesis procedure, 3 g of malonic acid was taken in a silica crucible and heated in the muffle furnace at 400 °C for 5 min. The brown product obtained was dissolved in a known volume of 10 mM tris buffer (pH = 8). The obtained CQDs solution was ultrasonicated for 1 h and then centrifugation was carried out at 3000 rpm for 15 min to separate out the larger or agglomerated nanoparticles from the CQDs solution. The aqueous solution was filtered and obtained clear and transparent CQDs solution was used for further studies and characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of cations with carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The as-synthesized CQDs dissolved in the aqueous medium were utilized as a fluorescent sensing probe for any unique specific interaction occurring with different cations. Initially, an aqueous stock solution of CQDs was prepared in tris buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0). Besides this, alkali and alkaline earth metal chloride solutions of different concentrations were also prepared in the same buffer.

Firstly, the fluorescence response of aqueous solubilized CQDs was checked in detail. A sharp fluorescence emission band was observed at 442 nm when the excitation wavelength of 380 nm was used for irradiation. This same excitation wavelength was used in all of the further studies. The fluorescence intensity value in the absence of metal chlorides was considered the initial intensity. The effect of different cations on CQDs fluorescence intensity was systematically studied by incubating them with a range of concentrations of metal chlorides. For this purpose, the above two stock solutions (i.e. CQD solution and salt solution) were mixed in a 1:1 ratio to get a desirable salt concentration. The mixture was then shaken properly and allowed to equilibrate for 5 min. After 5 min of incubation, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was recorded at the above mentioned wavelength. This procedure was repeated with different concentrations of all other metal chlorides one after the other to check their effect on the fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and chemical analysis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structural features of synthesized CQDs were examined by a number of techniques. Fig. 1a displays the TEM image of CQDs. The bright field images of CQDs are rarely distinct due to a lack of contrast between CQDs and the carbon-coated copper grid. However, it is revealed that the CQDs exhibit spherical morphology with diameters in the range of 7–12 nm. Furthermore, the absence of lattice fringes in the HRTEM image of CQDs (Fig. 1b) indicates their amorphous nature which is also reflected in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, provided in the inset showing diffused rings devoid of spots [35]. The obtained result was further confirmed from the XRD spectra.

Fig. 1c displays the XRD pattern of CQDs which shows a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 25.6° which is generally ascribed to highly disordered carbon atoms [31]. The corresponding interlayer spacing (d), which is estimated to be 0.347 nm, is close to that of bulk graphite (0.335 nm) [36]. The small difference observed in interlayer spacing of the bulk graphite and CQDs is attributed to the presence of functional groups such as OH, CH at the edges which can extend the spacing between graphene layers [37]. Moreover, the relatively broad nature of the obtained peak is due to partial graphitization and small size of synthesized CQDs [38,39]. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of CQDs shown in Fig. 1d reveals that the CQDs tend to agglomerate and appear as clusters [40,41]. Moreover, the elemental distribution was inspected using an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis. The EDX result depicts that carbon (68.9%) and oxygen (30.88%) are the dominant elements present in the CQDs with a small content of calcium (0.22%).

In order to identify various surface functional groups in CQDs, the FTIR spectrum of the aqueous solution of CQDs was recorded (Fig. 2 ). A broad absorption band at 3430 cm−1 is observed corresponding to OH stretching vibration and the broadness of the band suggests the presence of a large number of OH groups [29]. The peaks observed at 2928 cm−1 and 2817 cm−1 are due to CH asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations respectively [42]. CQDs exhibit an intense absorption peak at 1631 cm−1 which is assigned to CC stretching vibrations [43]. Two prominent peaks at 1591 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 are generally assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate anion (COO−) respectively [44]. The peak at 1351 cm−1 appears due to CH bending vibrations and a weak absorption shoulder at 1025 cm−1 is due to CO stretching vibrations [43]. The peak at 766 cm−1 can be attributed to CH out-of-plane bending vibrations [45]. These results suggest that the synthesized CQDs have a large number of acidic functional groups on the surface which enhances their hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous solutions [29,46].

To further investigate the surface of CQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed. The XPS survey scan (Fig. 3a) shows two characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV and 532.2 eV corresponding to C1s and O1s respectively [46], showing that the synthesized CQDs are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) can be further deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.9 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.7 eV corresponding to graphitic CC, COH/COC and OCO respectively [47]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) displays two peaks at 531.5 eV and 532.1 eV which can be assigned to CO and COH/COC respectively [48,49]. The XPS results are found to be consistent with the results obtained from the FTIR, revealing that the CQDs consist of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups.

The zeta potential of CQDs (Fig. 3d) was measured as −29.0 mV suggesting that they have good colloidal stability. The negative zeta potential value further confirms the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups at the CQDs surface [50,51].

The optical properties of as-synthesized CQDs were studied using UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. Fig. 4a shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs dispersed in 10 mM tris buffer (pH 8.0). It is observed that CQDs show a broad absorption band in the UV region with shoulders at 225 nm and 285 nm. These absorptions can be ascribed to π → π* transition of the conjugated CC band from the carbon core and n → π* transition arising due to the non-bonding electron orbitals of CO functional group at the CQD surface, respectively [52]. To further investigate the optical properties of synthesized CQDs, fluorescence studies were carried out. Fig. 4b shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs dispersed in 10 mM tris buffer (pH = 8.0) at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 350 nm to 440 nm. On increasing excitation wavelength in 10 nm increments, the fluorescence emission peak shows an expected red shift from 449 to 483 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity changes remarkably with varying excitation wavelengths and the maximum emission intensity is observed at 380 nm excitation. This clearly shows that the fluorescence emission is totally excitation dependent which is a unique feature of CQDs, unlike the traditional semiconductor quantum dots. The actual mechanism for fluorescence is still not clear but there are reports about the role of the surface states that exist on the CQDs surface. These surface states correspond to different functional groups on the surface. When the CQDs are irradiated, these surface states trap excitons and the energy is released in the form of photons. Thus, fluorescence emission may be attributed to the radiative recombination of excitons in the surface states which act as emission centers [53]. The corresponding emission centers would dominate at different excitation wavelengths, resulting in excitation-dependent fluorescence emission [54]. In addition, a plausible reason for the appearance of shoulders in the emission spectra is the presence of different types of chromophores in the CQDs which emits light at a slightly different emission wavelengths [55]. Moreover, the intensity of fluorescence relies on the number of molecules being excited at a specific wavelength. Thus, the reason for the maximum intensity observed at 380 nm is that the maximum number of molecules are getting excited at this irradiation wavelength [56]. This excitation wavelength was chosen to carry out all further fluorescence measurements of CQDs in the presence of different metal chlorides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence data of CQDs with chloride salts
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence intensity of CQDs solution in 10 mM tris buffer (pH 8.0) was measured as a function of varying concentrations of 10 different metal chloride salt solutions. Fig. 5 . shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of magnesium and lithium chloride salts. In both cases, sharp fluorescence enhancement was noticed as an increasing amount of salt was added to CQDs containing the aqueous solution. Since the CQDs are synthesized by the thermal decomposition of a simple dicarboxylic acid, malonic acid, with a pKa2 of 5.69, the nanomaterial surface will be negatively charged under the experimental conditions. Under alkaline conditions, the negative surface potential has been known to quench the fluorescence behavior of carbon nanomaterials [57]. Binding or partitioning of any positively charged ionic species to such surface should lead to fluorescence de-quenching or enhancement. Here also, the positively charged cations will interact with the carboxylate moiety on the CQDs surface forming ion pairs which should decrease the negative potential and enhance the fluorescence intensity. This increase in emission intensity is greater at low concentrations. Once the surface binding sites are saturated, the gap between the fluorescence intensities of adjoining concentrations becomes negligible. Beyond specific higher values of concentration i.e. 0.3 M for magnesium chloride and 3 M for lithium chloride, no further increment in fluorescence intensity was visible. Enhancement in emission intensity for magnesium ion was much greater than for monovalent lithium ion. Similar behavior was observed for other divalent and monovalent metal chloride salts.

Fig. 6 . illustrates that these partitioning and binding of cations to CQDs surface have a high degree of specificity. Magnesium and lithium ions showed the highest amount of increase in fluorescence intensity in their respective groups of divalent and monovalent cations. It should also be noted here that the fluorescence enhancement is much more pronounced in the case of divalent cations as compared to monovalent cations. Highly hydrophobic ions such as tetramethylammonium ion and cesium ion showed no effect on the fluorescence response of CQDs.

Fig. 7 displays the overall change in fluorescence intensity of CQDs with successive increments in concentration up to 0.5 M of divalent and 2 M of monovalent chloride salts. From the figure, it is observed that there is an exponential increase in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs solution with the addition of divalent salts up to 0.05 M. As the salt concentration is further increased, the fluorescence intensity increased much more gradually or became constant. Quite clearly, the partitioning affinity for the divalent salts for negatively charged nanomaterial surface is as follows: Mg2+ > Ca2+ > Sr2+ > Ba2+. Specifically, Mg2+ enhances the fluorescence intensity the most, while Ba2+ increases the least. Similarly, the order of increase in case of monovalent cations is: Li+ > NH4 + > K+≈ Na+ > Cs+ > Me4N+. For larger ions such as Cs+ and Me4N+, the amount of increase of fluorescence intensity is negligible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fitting of the fluorescence binding data
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence enhancement data of CQDs collected after incubation with different monovalent and divalent cations was fitted using modified Langmuir binding Eq. (1):
(1)
f =
B max
M
e
M
K d
+
M
e
M
where the constant Bmax, having units of fluorescence intensity, represents the maximum increase in the fluorescence intensity (f) and [M] is the molar salt concentration. The constant, Kd denotes dissociation constant of binding of cations with the CQD surface. The smaller the value of Kd, the greater should be the binding affinity. The fitting of the experimental data to Eq. (1) is represented as the dashed lines in Fig. 7. From the fitting of data for monovalent as well as divalent chloride salts, the values obtained for Kd and Bmax are summed up in Table 1 . The hydration enthalpy values for the cations are also displayed in the table.

It can be noted from the table that the Kd values for the divalent cations are much lower than those for monovalent cations. Besides, in the series of monovalent cations, the larger cations Cs+ and Me4N+ have the highest values of dissociation constant. This clearly indicates almost negligible binding of these ions to CQDs. The magnitude of the Bmax parameter for divalent cations is found to be in the range of 260 to 300 a.u. On the contrary, the corresponding number was only near 40 a.u. for monovalent cations with the exception of Li+ and NH4 +. For these cations, Bmax values are quite higher. This reveals that the fluorescence enhancement is the highest for these two ions in the monovalent cation series and magnesium among divalent cations.

This study clearly gives the proof that the presence of a substantial amount of negatively charged carboxylate anion groups on the CQDs surface is the supreme factor behind inducing specificity for cations. To further validate this hypothesis, the fluorescence measurements were also carried out in citrate buffer, pH 2.0. Nanomaterial acidic functional groups are expected to be protonated under such experimental conditions. As reflected in Fig. S1. (Supporting Information), fluorescence enhancement on incubation with both monovalent and divalent cations was very minimal. This clearly proves that a much weaker association takes place between protonated acidic groups and metal ions.

This study clearly gives the proof that the presence of a substantial amount of negatively charged carboxylate anion groups on the CQDs surface is the supreme factor behind inducing specificity for cations. To further validate this hypothesis, the fluorescence measurements were also carried out in citrate buffer, pH 2.0. Nanomaterial acidic functional groups are expected to be protonated under such experimental conditions. As reflected in Fig. S1. (Supporting Information), fluorescence enhancement on incubation with both monovalent and divalent cations was very minimal. This clearly proves that a much weaker association takes place between protonated acidic groups and metal ions.

The binding data obtained from fluorescence experiments in this study is summarized in Table 1. As can be clearly seen, the dissociation constant values for the divalent metal cations with the carboxylate containing surface of CQDs range from ~3 mM to ~1.3 M reflecting their greater binding affinity for negatively charged surface. Magnesium ion with the highest charge density showed the strongest association affinity with the nanomaterial surface. In comparison, Li+ exhibited the strongest binding affinity among the monovalent cations with dissociation constant value of ~1.8 M. Furthermore, the binding curves for the monovalent ions exhibited a saturation behavior at much higher concentrations. This was expected as the monovalent cations have a lower partitioning affinity for the charged interface. The specificity of these cation partitioning tendencies was tested via the law of matching water affinities (LMWA) [58]. This law makes provision for thermodynamically favored ion pairing between cations and anions having similar hydration enthalpies i.e. a stable ion pair will be formed if the difference between the hydration enthalpies of the ions is small [7,10]. This suggests that a kosmotropic or a chaotropic cation should have tendency to pair with a kosmotropic or a chaotropic anion respectively. So, higher affinity of carboxylate anions on the CQDs to similarly hydrated cations is expected. For the justification of this hypothesis, the recorded Kd values were plotted against the difference in hydration enthalpy of cations and acetate anion i.e. ΔHhyd(Cation-Acetate). All of the hydration enthalpy data utilized in making this correlation plot is given in Table 1.

The deviation of Li+ and NH4 + from the usual LMWA rule is a consequence of a number of possible reasons. Li+, due to its much smaller size and greater charge density as compared to other monovalent cations, interact more firmly than the other cations [7]. Besides, NH4 + does not fit into the correlation data due to the reason that it can pair with acetate ion through hydrogen bonding in addition to overall electrostatic interactions [59,60]. Hydrogen bonding occurs between positively charged ammonium cation acting as hydrogen bond donor and oxygen atoms of acetate anion as strong acceptor. In contrast, tetramethylammonium cation has no proton associated with the nitrogen due to which it is incapable of forming hydrogen bonds with acetate anion and thus, follows the LMWA rule. Interaction of divalent cations with acetate moiety doesn't seem to follow the LMWA explanation. This seems to indicate that the divalent ions may be interacting with the nanomaterial surface via their hydrated form.

Lastly, it is also worth noting that the CQDs consist of aromatic π-system in the core. So the non-covalent cation-π interactions between the cation monopole and the electric quadrupole of the aromatic system may also be present [61,62]. These interactions might have some role to play in binding of cations to the CQDs surface. However, specific cation partitioning and binding to molecular surfaces is clearly dominated by the nature and number density of acidic functional groups. This idea should also lead to better molecular designs for sensing heavy metal ions in industrial waste water.

A novel and highly effective fluorescence based methodology has been tested here for investigating the mechanism of cationic lyotropic series. Synthesized CQDs showed striking selective enhancement of fluorescence on incubation with different chloride salts. This strategy of a simple, water-soluble and fluorescent nanomaterial holds great promise in deciphering the significance of every functional moiety in biochemical interactions.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
Fluorescence response of CQDs with (a) 100 mM concentration of divalent and (b) 300 mM of monovalent chloride salts. All the salt solutions, as well as CQDs solution, were prepared in 10 mM citrate buffer, pH 2.0.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2020.113086.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Shameema Rana: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Jaibir Kherb: Supervision, Validation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by funds from the National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur (HP). The authors would also like to acknowledge the support of Dept. of Material Science & Engineering (DMSE) and Centre for Energy & Environment Engineering (CEEE) at the National Institute of Technology, Hamirpur (HP) for providing their help with the sample characterization during this research work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanistic insights into metal ions transit through threefold ferritin channel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Chandramouli, S. Del Galdo, G. Mancini, V. Barone, Mechanistic insights into metal ions transit through threefold ferritin channel, Biochim. Biophys. Acta - Gen. Subj. 1863 (2019) 472–480. doi:10.1016/j.bbagen.2018.11.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigations of the Hofmeister series and other specific ion effects using lipid model systems

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Leontidis, Investigations of the Hofmeister series and other specific ion effects using lipid model systems, Adv. Colloid Interface Sci. 243 (2017) 8–22. doi:10.1016/j.cis.2017.04.001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ion specific influences on the stability and unfolding transitions of a naturally aggregating protein; RecA

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.R. Cannon, N.D. Talley, B.A. Danzig, X. Liu, J.S. Martinez, A.P. Shreve, G. MacDonald, Ion specific influences on the stability and unfolding transitions of a naturally aggregating protein; RecA, Biophys. Chem. 163–164 (2012) 56–63. doi:10.1016/j.bpc.2012.02.005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Correlation of lysozyme activity and stability in the presence of Hofmeister series anions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Garajova, A. Balogova, E. Dusekova, D. Sedlakova, E. Sedlak, R. Varhac, Correlation of lysozyme activity and stability in the presence of Hofmeister series anions, Biochim. Biophys. Acta - Proteins Proteomics. 1865 (2017) 281–288. doi:10.1016/j.bbapap.2016.11.016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemistry: getting specific about specific ion effects
